{"path": "workspace/python_guide.txt", "content": "================================================================================
                    PYTHON BEST PRACTICES & TESTING GUIDE
================================================================================

TABLE OF CONTENTS
-----------------
PART I:   PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
  1.1  Code Style & Formatting (PEP 8)
  1.2  Project Structure
  1.3  Naming Conventions
  1.4  Documentation & Type Hints
  1.5  Error Handling
  1.6  Performance & Idiomatic Python
  1.7  Dependency & Environment Management
  1.8  Security Best Practices

PART II:  PYTHON TESTING FRAMEWORKS
  2.1  Overview of Major Frameworks
  2.2  pytest (Recommended)
  2.3  unittest (Standard Library)
  2.4  nose2
  2.5  doctest
  2.6  tox & nox (Test Orchestration)
  2.7  Mocking & Fixtures
  2.8  Choosing the Right Framework

================================================================================
PART I: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
-----------------------------------
- Use 4 spaces per indentation level (never tabs).
- Limit lines to 79 characters for code, 72 for docstrings/comments.
- Surround top-level function and class definitions with two blank lines.
- Method definitions inside a class are surrounded by a single blank line.
- Imports should be on separate lines and grouped in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Related third-party imports
    3. Local application/library-specific imports
- Use absolute imports: `from mypackage import mymodule`
- Avoid wildcard imports (`from module import *`).

Automated Tools:
  - black    — Opinionated code formatter (run: `black .`)
  - ruff     — Fast linter + formatter (run: `ruff check .`)
  - isort    — Import sorter (run: `isort .`)
  - flake8   — Linter combining pycodestyle, pyflakes, mccabe
  - mypy     — Static type checker

1.2 PROJECT STRUCTURE
---------------------
A well-organized Python project typically follows this layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern build config (or setup.cfg)
    ├── LICENSE
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module_a.py
    │       └── module_b.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module_a.py
    │   └── test_module_b.py
    ├── docs/
    └── scripts/

Key principles:
  - Use a `src/` layout to prevent accidental imports of the package under
    development.
  - Keep tests in a separate `tests/` directory mirroring the source structure.
  - Use `pyproject.toml` as the single source of project metadata and tool
    configuration.

1.3 NAMING CONVENTIONS
----------------------
  Type                  | Convention              | Example
  ----------------------|-------------------------|-------------------
  Modules               | lowercase_underscore    | data_processor.py
  Packages              | lowercase (short)       | mypackage
  Classes               | CapitalizedWords        | DataProcessor
  Functions / Methods   | lowercase_underscore    | process_data()
  Constants             | UPPER_CASE_UNDERSCORE   | MAX_RETRIES = 5
  Variables             | lowercase_underscore    | user_count
  Private (internal)    | _leading_underscore     | _internal_helper()
  "Magic" / dunder      | __double_underscore__   | __init__, __str__
  Type variables        | CapitalizedWords        | T, UserType

- Avoid single-letter names except in comprehensions or loop counters.
- Use verbs for functions (`get_user`, `calculate_total`), nouns for classes.

1.4 DOCUMENTATION & TYPE HINTS
------------------------------
- Every public module, class, function, and method should have a docstring.
- Use one of these docstring styles consistently:
    - Google style (recommended for readability)
    - Num

------------------------------
- Every public module, class, function, and method should have a docstring.
- Use one of these docstring styles consistently:
    - Google style (recommended for readability)
    - NumPy/SciPy style

                    PYTHON BEST PRACTICES & TESTING GUIDE
================================================================================

TABLE OF CONTENTS

PART I:   PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
  1.1  Code Style & Formatting (PEP 8)
  1.2  Project Structure
  1.3  Naming Conventions
  1.4  Documentation & Type Hints
  1.5  Error Handling

  1.7  Dependency & Environment Management
  1.8  Security Best Practices

PART II:  PYTHON TESTING FRAMEWORKS
  2.1  Overview of Major Frameworks
  2.2  pytest (Recommended)
  2.3  unittest (Standard Library)
  2.4  nose2
  2.5  doctest
  2.6  tox & nox (Test Orchestration)
  2.7  Mocking & Fixtures
  2.8  Choosing the Right Framework

================================================================================
PART I: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
-----------------------------------
- Use 4 spaces per indentation level (never tabs).
- Limit lines to 79 characters for code, 72 for docstrings/comments.
- Surround top-level function and class definitions with two blank lines.
- Method definitions inside a class are surrounded by a single blank line.
- Imports should be on separate lines and grouped in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Related third-party imports
    3. Local application/library-specific imports
- Use absolute imports: `from mypackage import mymodule`
- Avoid wildcard imports (`from module import *`).

Automated Tools:
  - black    — Opinionated code formatter (run: `black .`)
  - ruff     — Fast linter + formatter (run: `ruff check .`)
  - isort    — Import sorter (run: `isort .`)
  - flake8   — Linter combining pycodestyle, pyflakes, mccabe
  - mypy     — Static type checker

1.2 PROJECT STRUCTURE
---------------------
A well-organized Python project typically follows this layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern build config
    ├── LICENSE
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module_a.py
    │       └── module_b.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module_a.py
    │   └── test_module_b.py
    ├── docs/
    └── scripts/

Key principles:
  - Use a `src/` layout to prevent accidental imports of the package under
    development.
  - Keep tests in a separate `tests/` directory mirroring the source structure.
  - Use `pyproject.toml` as the single source of project metadata and tool
    configuration.

1.3 NAMING CONVENTIONS
----------------------
  Type                  | Convention              | Example
  ----------------------|-------------------------|-------------------
  Modules               | lowercase_underscore    | data_processor.py
  Packages              | lowercase (short)       | mypackage
  Classes               | CapitalizedWords        | DataProcessor
  Functions / Methods   | lowercase_underscore    | process_data()
  Constants             | UPPER_CASE_UNDERSCORE   | MAX_RETRIES = 5
  Variables             | lowercase_underscore    | user_count
  Private (internal)    | _leading_underscore     | _internal_helper()
  "Magic" / dunder      | __double_underscore__   | __init__, __str__
  Type variables        | CapitalizedWords        | T, UserType

- Avoid single-letter names except in comprehensions or loop counters.
- Use verbs for functions (`get_user`, `calculate_total`), nouns for classes.
